All right, division of business roles. Okay, so this is with either your spouse or your partner, right? Here's how you're going to do this. If right now you're in a spot where you feel like there is no clear division between responsibilities and you are looking at restructuring your team and implementing everything in this portal, then here's what you need to do first. Okay, one is you want to write down a list of the business management needs. Okay, so what things need to be managed and overseen? From my perspective, you've got general management, right, of people. You've got finance, you've got marketing, you've got sales, HR slash payroll, which is employee issues and paying them, right? And then you've got legal compliance, which kind of rolls into HR and payroll, right? So you want to write all of those down. Those are the functions of the business, right? After you've written them down, you want to write down where the gaps lie based on the job descriptions, which you already have for each role, right? So say that you have a trainer or three trainers and an admin, right? And then you want to look at the functions and say, are they covering any of these? And if so, which ones? And then looking at the rest and saying, am I covering these? Is my spouse covering some of these? Okay, so then categorizing with who you have now, what they're overseeing, right? Three is you want to identify the skills or requirements needed for each role as well as the responsibilities that are performed on a day-to-day basis. Okay? So if you are looking at it and you see that your trainers have, I don't know, HR and payroll, so your head trainer is doing that and you feel like they suck all the time, well, does the head trainer have the knowledge or expertise to be doing payroll and to be doing HR? Probably not. Again, with finance, say you and your spouse run the business together and you are looking at who oversees finance, but maybe you oversee finance, but you are not good with numbers, but your spouse is. You want to align your skills. So maybe your spouse needs to take on finance, right? Same goes for marketing. Typically, marketing and sales can be rolled under one spouse while I would say the rest of these can be rolled under the other because typically there's one person who is more operationally sound. If one of you is not more operationally sound than one of you needs to learn. Okay, so once you've done this and you've identified, here's what we have as needs, here's what it looks like present day and here is where things don't make sense, where people are doing things they shouldn't do, right? Then you need to start discussing this. First, who of the two of you has the skills and if neither of you do. So first making a block of like spouse number one, spouse number two, skills, skills, right? And then from there, you want to look at the functions you have in your business and assign those functions to the spouse that has the skill, right? So first you want to define skill of each spouse and then assign skill that represents the function of the business. Cool. Then you want to look at where the gaps lie. So where is it that neither of you have the skill or the time? And then those are the positions that you want to hire for first. So for Alex and I, we divided up the business and one of the first hires that we made was tech support because as we went through, it was very apparent that Alex need to be in charge of marketing and sales. When we first started, I would be in charge of all of operations and then it was like, Oh, well, I actually, even though I'm in charge of operations, what's the thing I suck at the most that we need? And I was like tech support crap, right? So that was the first position I hired for and then I hired two more. Okay. And so that's what you delegate first is your weaknesses. However, what I would like to just emphasize is that though you're delegating your weaknesses, you need to know what it looks like when it's done well. So for me, I always have to have some knowledge or understanding over the tech, over the customer support, over the marketing. I need to have a little bit of knowledge about everything. And as a business owner, even if you don't like something, you have to come to the conclusion and accept the fact that you have to know a little bit about everything, even if you don't like it. Okay. Example for that could also be reading a P and L statement. Do I like reading a P and L statement? Fuck no. Do I knew how do I need to know how to make one? No, I can pay someone $50 to do one. But do I need to know how to read one and how to spot if one is wrong? Yes. Okay. As is something that you should know. Four is that then you need to develop your four Rs for both of you in writing and keep that on hand at all times. And so in the next section, I'm going to go over what four Rs are. They're basically your job description and each of you is incredibly important that you have them for yourselves so that not only can you hold each other accountable to this third piece of information, but that your entire team knows who to go to for what to eliminate confusion when it comes to communication.
